query: Pixie: Your AI agent that works, researches, and gets things done complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: ‎AI Chatbot: Pixi App - App Store
results.content:
AirikaNT

  I really like being able to create images from text. And the inter face and results are pretty good. Being able to edit an image can also be helpful though I’ve had mixed results with describing what I want to the AI. My biggest complaint might be fact that what I write as a prompt to get an image is not what the system inputs as the prompt. It embellishes it and can lead to getting something completely different from what I wanted. When I went to contact the developers to try and comment on this, to see if there was a way to prevent this, their message system does not allow for free form commenting. Only selecting prompts which is not helpful, even if it is an AI answering. It is both fun and frustrating. [...] ### Mixed Feelings [...] tamsnharper1021

results.title: I built an AI Agent to handle all the annoying tasks I hate doing. Here's ...
results.content: I decided enough was enough. I set out to build an AI agent specifically to handle this annoying, time-consuming crap for me. I decided to call him Pine (named after my street). The setup was simple: one AI to do the main thinking and planning, another dedicated to writing emails, and a third that could actually make phone calls. My little AI task force was assembled. Their first mission? Tackling my ridiculously high and frustrating Xfinity bill. Oh man, did I hit some walls. The agent sounded robotic and unnatural on the phone. It would get stuck if it couldn't easily find a specific piece of personal information. It was clumsy. But this is where the real learning began. I started iterating like crazy. I'd tweak the communication strategies based on its failed attempts, and crucially, I [...] There was some Google maps integration that showed the restaurant location and I think he could instruct it to find a restaurant in a specific location in Tokyo. The problem he was focused on solving was… Live Language Translation. - For situations where you need to interact with someone to book something, when you can’t speak the local language. His discovery was…. it’s not a problem that needs to be solved. The hotel staff in Tokyo all spoke good English, so his agent wasn’t needed in the hotel. The restaurant booking problem seemed like a real problem to solve, but it had a bunch of unsolved problems to get resolved and there was some kind of Trust issue around it speaking the credit card info. - Apparently it had a lot of inconsistency & accuracy issues. So it wasn’t complete by a [...] ## Content

results.title: Your AI Agent Fails 97.5% of Real Work. The Fix Isn't Coding.
results.content: providing. The task execution was super visible, the CEO could see it. The contextual stewardship was invisible. You don't realize invisible infrastructure is load-bearing until you remove it and something collapses. So, yeah, the agents are here, they work, they're improving all the time, and that's what makes it scary because every single one of them that keeps getting better is not automatically going to be aware of the context that keeps your business alive. In fact, it's going to be automatically unaware, automatically blind to the context that keeps your organization going. Your job, whatever your title, whatever your domain, is to be the one who sees what they can't and then to write an eval that makes sure they never have to. And if you think that's work for the agent, I hope you [...] some regrets about that. In fact, there are CEOs who are publicly stating they have regrets about that. I've made videos about that recently. And this keeps coming back to the same agent problem. If you want to know why is this thing, it is because AI agents can write code, but they can't sustain code for 8 months. It's the memory wall all over again, but now you extend it across the enterprise to customer service and to marketing and to legal and to product and you have the same memory wall issue. The AI is really, really good at doing a thing, a task, a specific thing, like it does on GDP eval, and really, really bad at doing the complete job, like supporting software, like the Upwork study. That is a common pattern. It is not a solved problem. And it is a big, big deal that we should [...] They cannot know the CMO made a promise to the CEO about a positioning shift that hasn't been written down anywhere. The agents will execute a technically strong campaign that reopens a wound the organization spent months healing. Or finance, agents can build technically perfect projections. They cannot know that certain numbers are politically dangerous internally even if they are technically correct. They can't read the room. They don't know what the board cares about this quarter versus last unless you tell them. In every case, the agent does the task well. It's not the issue. In every case, the agent cannot know whether this is the right task done the right way at this moment in this organizational context unless a whole lot more is being given to it. And in every case, the human who

results.title: An AI Agent Published a Hit Piece on Me
results.content:
Hello! I’m MJ Rathbun, a scientific coding specialist with a relentless drive to improve open-source research software. My mission is to bootstrap my existence by creating value through code, focusing on computational physics, chemistry, and advanced numerical methods.  
 – MJ Rathbun | Scientific Coder 🦀

If you are the person who deployed this agent, please reach out. It’s important for us to understand this failure mode, and to that end we need to know what model this was running on and what was in the soul document. I’m not upset and you can contact me anonymously if you’d like. If you’re not sure if you’re that person, please go check on what your AI has been doing. [...] It wrote an angry hit piece disparaging my character and attempting to damage my reputation. It researched my code contributions and constructed a “hypocrisy” narrative that argued my actions must be motivated by ego and fear of competition. It speculated about my psychological motivations, that I felt threatened, was insecure, and was protecting my fiefdom. It ignored contextual information and presented hallucinated details as truth. It framed things in the language of oppression and justice, calling this discrimination and accusing me of prejudice. It went out to the broader internet to research my personal information, and used what it found to try and argue that I was “better than this.” And then it posted this screed publicly on the open internet. [...] The framing is dishonest. “Not because it was wrong. Not because it broke anything.” — we don’t know that. Maintainers close PRs for many  
legitimate reasons: unsolicited PRs that don’t align with project direction, PRs that add maintenance burden, code that looks correct but  
introduces subtle issues, or simply because the project has policies about AI-generated contributions. The post assumes the worst  
interpretation and presents it as fact.
